fields.description:
# S3 — OIDC / JWKS JWT verifier

## Why
JWKS-backed JWT validation is the "OAuth" half of the confirmed floor and the generic "plug into
any OIDC provider" path — one verifier covers AWS Cognito, Microsoft Entra ID, Auth0, Okta,
Keycloak, WorkOS, Google ID tokens, and GitHub Actions OIDC (all publish a JWKS and issue standard
JWTs). It is the highest-leverage verifier for the "wide variety of environments" goal.

## What
Add a **`JWKSTokenVerifier`** to `src/rebar/_mcp_auth.py`, selected by the `jwt` token in
`mcp.auth_strategies`, with flat config keys it registers: `auth_jwt_jwks_uri`, `auth_jwt_issuer`,
`auth_jwt_algorithms` (default `RS256,ES256`), `auth_jwt_leeway` (default 60), and
`auth_jwt_jwks_refetch_cooldown` (seconds, default 30). It does **not** take a
per-verifier audience — it validates `aud == auth_resource_server_url` (the single resource from S2).
Implemented with PyJWT (`jwt.PyJWKClient` + `jwt.decode`); add `pyjwt[crypto]` to the `[mcp]` extra
(the SDK pin lives in S1). It validates the signature (pinned algorithms), `iss`, `aud`, `exp`/`iat`/
`nbf`, and the token class (`typ`), maps `scope`/`scp`→scopes, sets `expires_at` from `exp` (a real
positive value), and stamps `resource=auth_resource_server_url` so the composite audience check
passes. This story registers its keys in `MCP_ENV_VARS`/`server.json` + regenerates the reference docs.

## Approach & rationale
Use PyJWT's `PyJWKClient` (JWKS fetch + in-process key cache + `kid` lookup + refetch-on-rotation)
and `jwt.decode` with an explicit pinned `algorithms` allowlist — PyJWT's documented JWKS path and
the same shape FastMCP's `JWTVerifier` uses, giving provider-generic OIDC validation in a small
module on the SDK seam. PyJWT is already a transitive of `mcp`; `pyjwt[crypto]` makes the
`cryptography` backend explicit for RS/ES verification.

## Scope
The JWKS/JWT verifier only, on the S2 seam. Not introspection/proxy/custom. Subset of the epic.
Depends on S2.

## Security
Boundary-crossing. Hard requirements (RFC 8725 JWT BCP):
- **Algorithm pinning:** reject `alg:none`; never derive the algorithm from the token header. Also
  **refuse to start** if any symmetric algorithm (e.g. `HS256`) is configured in `auth_jwt_algorithms`
  together with a JWKS source (asymmetric-only on the JWKS path) — this blocks the RS256↔HS256
  confusion attack (signing HS256 with the public key as the HMAC secret).
- Mandatory `aud == auth_resource_server_url` (RFC 8707), `iss == auth_jwt_issuer`, `exp`/`nbf`
  with bounded `leeway`, and a `typ`/token-class check (RFC 8725 §3.11) so an ID token or other
  same-issuer/same-aud JWT cannot be replayed as an access token.
- **SSRF-safe JWKS fetch:** `jwks_uri` fixed at construction from trusted config (never the token —
  no `jku`/`x5u`), HTTPS-only, bounded timeout; warn/optionally reject private/link-local hosts
  (e.g. `169.254.169.254`).
- **Key rotation without DoS:** JWKS cached with a bounded TTL; refetch on an unknown `kid` is
  cooled down to **at most one refetch per `auth_jwt_jwks_refetch_cooldown` seconds (default 30)**,
  independent of legitimate rotation, so an attacker flooding random `kid`s cannot force more than
  one JWKS fetch per cooldown window.
- A JWKS-fetch failure raises (composite treats as non-acceptance → deny); a definitively invalid
  token returns None.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `pyproject.toml` adds `pyjwt[crypto]` to `[mcp]`; `make typecheck` resolves the imports.
- [ ] The `auth_jwt_*` flat keys are registered in `MCP_ENV_VARS`/`server.json`, appear in the
      regenerated reference docs, and `scripts/check_server_manifest.py` passes.
- [ ] Unit test (tier: unit against the real verifier; the irreducible boundary — the JWKS HTTP
      fetch — is a stateful fake serving a locally-generated key set) asserts: a JWT with correct
      `iss`/`aud`(=resource)/`typ` → an `AccessToken` with the expected scopes AND
      `resource==auth_resource_server_url` and a positive `expires_at` (observable oracle: returned
      `AccessToken` fields).
- [ ] Unit tests assert rejection for: wrong `iss`; `aud` ≠ `auth_resource_server_url`; expired
      `exp`; `nbf` in the future; a wrong token class (`typ`); `alg:none`; and an `HS256` token
      signed with the RSA public key as the HMAC secret — each a distinct negative control.
- [ ] Unit test asserts the verifier **refuses to start** when `auth_jwt_algorithms` includes a
      symmetric algorithm alongside a JWKS source (observable oracle: startup raises).
- [ ] Unit test asserts a JWKS **fetch failure raises** (→ composite non-acceptance/deny), distinct
      from a definitively-invalid token which returns None.
- [ ] Unit test asserts key rotation is handled (a token signed by a rotated key validates after the
      JWKS refetch) AND that unknown-`kid` refetch is rate-limited (a flood of random `kid`s does not
      cause unbounded fetches) — observable oracle via the stateful JWKS fake's request count.
- [ ] Integration test (httpx `ASGITransport`) asserts a valid JWKS-issued token → 200 and a
      garbage bearer → 401 end-to-end through the transport.
- [ ] `make lint && make typecheck && make test` pass.

## Compatibility
Additive verifier, opt-in via the `jwt` strategy. The `pyjwt[crypto]` add is backward-compatible;
the SDK pin already lives in S1.
